Mistake, delivering overly sophisticated or complex content. You can't ask them to think a lot. They don't like it. Diagram with five things on it, okay. Diagram with 15 things on it, too much. I just don't want to do that. It is too much. So if you're too complex, if you're too sophisticated, especially too soon, or with the wrong people who haven't moved up through an essential ladder, you'll drive them away. You'll lose them for all the same reasons. They're embarrassed. They don't understand what they're hearing or reading, but they won't ask. And so now they're not getting value. They feel like a slow student in the room, et cetera. Teaching too much, period. The worst thing in the world you can do in a coaching environment is fall in love with the sound of your own voice. Not why they're there. They may have joined because of you, but that's not what's going to keep them there. They can only take so much, even in a seminar environment. And I tend to crank it up, say, I just pay a lot of money to be here. So I think, well, you know, what's working to death, but you're going to be happy if I work it again. Ten years ago I would have gone until midnight tonight. Now I know. I could go until midnight. You'll be less happy even if you've got another whole big notebook and we went until midnight. Mostly you'll be less happy, not more happy, even though you can sleep when you go home for free. You'll be less happy. They only take so much. And then it's too much. And then everybody's unhappy. So teaching too much, period. Turns into boredom, drudgery. People got to have fun, especially in the coaching environment. They got to have fun. One way versus cafeteria. You lose a lot of people in coaching. We used to lose them in practice management if you have one way for them to do something. Here's my successful way. You're going to do it my way. They're happier if you give them a cafeteria. And so they can pick and choose stuff. Now that's not the most effective thing usually you can do for them. I'm going to do you guys after the break. Is that okay? So yeah. So be up here after the break, okay? So usually the most effective thing we can do is give them an absolute model. Conform to this model, boom, you get these results. But that's not what they want. They're not going to be happy about that. And to be fair, different people, different things. It's like that office tour thing. Different offices, different things. It's got to now be tweaked a little bit. Office by office by office by office. Some offices it ought to be the front desk person doing the tour because she's good and bubbly and personable and doesn't mind learning the script and will do a good job with it. But other offices, the worst person in the world you can have do the office is Barbara at the front desk. Barbara does a pretty good job at the front desk, but you know, Barbara don't like giving to her. And she's not going to like giving to her. And so you can't dogmatically say, tour should be given by main front desk person. That's how we do it. That's how everybody does it. Imposing your values on others. We kind of talked about that some. It's about their perceived value, not yours. This is actually a very useful list. It's kind of a dumb list, but it's a very useful list. It's worth flying across the country for. Here's the things that make them happy. Getting lots of stuff. They like stuff. They may never look at it. They may never use it. They like stuff. My coaching group members, when we had the VIP meetings in the house, you know what they like best? Getting in my way. They like my trash. Oh, God, they love the trash. I never took the trash. Once I figured it out, I didn't take the trash out before the meetings. Normally, you clean up. I made a bigger mess. God, they love getting in my trash. What can we find in Dan's trash? Oh, can I get a copy of what you just found in the trash? Can I get a copy of what you just found in the trash? They love what was in my trash. I filled the trash up for them. They love stuff. The platinum meetings, everybody brings stuff. Everybody hands out stuff. Everybody goes home with stuff. They love stuff. They give them a lot of stuff. They love it. They like taking home stuff. They feel like they went out. They killed something. They brought it back. They only go home empty-handed. They come home from a meeting. Somebody says, where were you? I was at the meeting. What'd you get? Where'd you go? I was at a meeting. What'd you get? I got all this stuff. Look at all this stuff. Great. They're happy. They like getting stuff in packages. Huge mistake. I just dealt with a client again, just about did themselves in, switch into electronic delivery of all of their content. Big mistake. Save a lot of money. Save a lot of money by sending that newsletter out electronically or letting you go download it in a PDF file. No good.
